title: Chasing a Moving Target
concept: Since the generator's target (fooling the discriminator) keeps changing as the discriminator itself improves, and vice versa, neither network has a fixed goalpost it can settle into.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What did we call it when generator and discriminator performance rise and fall in a repeating pattern instead of settling down?
exercises.explain: Right — oscillation. Today: the deeper reason it happens — a moving target.

cards.title: A finish line that runs away from you
cards.body: Imagine sprinting toward a finish line — except every time you get close, someone moves it. That's roughly what it's like to be a generator (or a discriminator) in GAN training.

cards.title: Why the target moves
cards.body: The generator's goal is to fool the current discriminator. But the discriminator is being trained too — it keeps getting better (or sometimes worse) at spotting fakes. So 'fool the discriminator' means something different at epoch 50 than it did at epoch 10. The generator is optimizing against a target that never holds still.

cards.title: Running up the down escalator
cards.body: Picture running up an escalator that's moving down. You can be making real effort and real progress with every step, yet your position relative to the top keeps depending on how fast the escalator is currently moving. Speed up? You gain ground. Escalator quickens? You lose ground — even though you never stopped climbing.

cards.title: Both sides are the moving target
cards.body: It's not just the generator chasing a moving discriminator — it's mutual. The discriminator's job is to tell real from fake, but 'fake' means whatever the generator is currently producing, and that keeps changing too. Each network is training against a constantly-shifting opponent, not a fixed, known target.

cards.title: This isn't a bug to be patched away
cards.body: It's tempting to think this moving-target problem is a mistake in how GANs are built. It's not — it's baked into the adversarial setup itself. Two networks training against each other's ever-changing outputs will always have this property to some degree, no matter how carefully you code it.

left.text:
Correct label is fixed
Target never changes
Model settles toward it

right.text:
'Fooling' the discriminator
Discriminator keeps changing
Target keeps shifting

exercises.prompt: Why does the generator's 'goal' keep changing during GAN training?

options.text: Because the discriminator it's trying to fool is itself being updated and improving/changing over time
Because the training dataset is randomly replaced every epoch
Because the generator's architecture is redesigned partway through training
Because the loss function is randomly swapped out

exercises.explain: The generator's target is 'fool the current discriminator' — and the discriminator keeps changing, so the target keeps moving.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Only the generator faces a moving target — the discriminator's job (tell real from fake) stays fixed throughout training.
exercises.explain: False — it's mutual. The discriminator has to keep up with a generator whose outputs keep changing too.

exercises.sentence: Because the generator and discriminator are each trained against each other's constantly ___ behavior, neither one gets a fixed, unmoving target to optimize toward.
exercises.explain: Each network's target is defined by the other network's current (changing) behavior.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best illustrates the 'moving target' problem in GAN training?

options.text: A generator learns a trick that fools the discriminator at epoch 20, but by epoch 40 the discriminator has adapted and that same trick no longer works
A classifier trained on labeled photos of cats and dogs, where 'cat' always means the same thing throughout training
A generator that is trained once and never updated again

exercises.explain: The discriminator adapting to a trick that used to work — shifting what 'success' means for the generator — is exactly the moving-target problem.

exercises.prompt: Both networks are chasing moving targets defined by each other. Given that, what would you expect a GAN's loss curves to look like on a graph — compared to a typical classifier's loss curve?

options.text: Bouncing up and down over time, rather than smoothly dropping to a flat, stable line
Identical to a typical classifier's curve, since all neural networks train the same way
A perfectly straight horizontal line from epoch one
Always trending toward zero with no exceptions

exercises.explain: Right — chasing a moving target produces bouncing, not smooth convergence. Next up: how to actually read those bouncing curves.
